Checklist

- [x] Appropriate tests were added
- [x] Any code changes were done in a way that does not break public API
- [x] All documentation related to code changes were updated
- [x] The new code follows the
  [contributor guidelines](https://github.com/SciML/.github/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md), in particular the [SciML Style Guide](https://github.com/SciML/SciMLStyle) and
  [COLPRAC](https://github.com/SciML/COLPRAC).

## Additional context

Would close #790 by adding a new constructor `OptimizationProblem(model::AbstractNLPModel, ...)` that makes the problem defined as an `NLPModel` available as an `OptimizationProblem` and thus, makes it available to the rest of the SciML ecosystem

So far the appropriate tests are still missing and the public API documentation hasn't been updated

@jbytecode With the last PR now merged, @pierre-borie answered all my comments and I completed the check-list. I am happy to recommend the publication of Enlsip in JOSS. The nonlinear least squares problem is both important and very frequent in practice, so I think this algorithm will be an interesting addition to the Julia background. My only comment would be that modeling a problem for the solver is not an easy task. I would recommend relying on NLPModels.jl to interact with the model as it would open the door to several modeling and benchmarking tools, but that's a biased opinion and independent of this submission process. Congrats @pierre-borie for the nice work!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2111138701/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The package looks very nice now, congrats. I still have a couple of comments. - [ ] As asked above, https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/4129#issuecomment-1287949731 , I don't think it is a good idea to only focus on the master simply because there is a reason we use versioning. The user needs to rely on versions that are stable and working especially since the package has a lot of deps. Staying up-to-date with the master is great but only for developers that are actively using the package. The following two would solve the issue: - [ ] Add link to `dev` and `stable` docs to the readme https://github.com/cgrudz/DataAssimilationBenchmarks.jl/issues/29 - [ ] Show how to install the stable version of the package - [ ] Regarding the automated tests, it passes on my computer. However, I am slightly concerned about the code coverage as only 40% of the code is tested. role of NLPModels.jl is to define an API that users and solvers can rely on. It is the role of other packages to implement facilities that create models compliant with the NLPModels API. There are several examples of this in JSO: AMPLNLReader allows users to connect the AMPL modeling language with NLPModels, CUTEst does the same for the SIF language, NLPModelsJuMP does the same with JuMP models, etc. In those three examples, there exist underlying modeling tools (in Julia or not). PDENLPModels is different in that there is no existing generic interface for optimization with PDEs. Instead, PDENLPModels interacts with Gridap to evaluate functionals and differential operators based on a discretization. Gridap in itself does not let users model optimization problems; only systems of PDEs. PDENLPModels provides all the extra facilities for users and solvers to interact with a PDE-constrained optimization problem as they would with a JuMP model, an AMPL model, or any other model that complies with the NLPModels API. To answer your three questions: - Both PDENLPModels and Gridap handle the modeling. PDENLPModels interacts with Gridap to evaluate functional/differential objects and their derivatives. However, in addition, PDENLPModels extends the differentiation capabilities of Gridap to address the requirements of optimization solvers, and allows finite-dimensional variables in the problem. - NLPModels only states the API that we conform to. It's the "syntax" of models, so to speak. - A solver only needs to worry about the NLPModels API and need never know where a model comes from; all the details are handled inside the model itself. In this instance, PDENLPModels encapsulates the details and interacts with Gridap when necessary. The solver never needs to know about Gridap. Indeed the same solver can be used to solve an AmplModel, a CUTEstModel, a MathProgNLPModel, etc. We would be happy to add the above clarifications to the paper if you deem it necessary.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/1320256067/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The link to the new tutorial: https://juliasmoothoptimizers.github.io/DCISolver.jl/dev/example/ @jbcaillau to re-run the benchmark from the paper, you can: - clone the repository - move to the branch `joss-paper-branch` ; then move to the `paper` folder. - run `benchmark.jl` which will generate a .jld2 file with the result, and finally run `figures.jl` to generate the figures.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/1015604065/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
